skill.name: firebase-data-connect
skill.description: Build Firebase Data Connect backends with PostgreSQL, GraphQL schemas, authorization rules, Firebase CLI workflows, and generated client SDKs.
skill.summary: Create secure Firebase Data Connect schemas, operations, and SDK integrations.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many matches because the skill is mostly Markdown documentation with GraphQL, YAML, TypeScript, and shell examples. I found no evidence of prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or hidden executable code, but the skill does guide Firebase CLI deployment, emulator use, SDK generation, and Admin SDK operations that can affect real projects.

medium_findings.title: Operational Firebase CLI Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Firebase CLI commands for initialization, emulator startup, SDK generation, and production deployment. These commands are legitimate for Firebase Data Connect work, but users should review project IDs and deployment targets before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and include production deployment. They are not hidden or malicious, but they can modify Firebase resources if run against the wrong project.

medium_findings.title: Admin SDK Bypasses Application Authorization
medium_findings.description: The SDK reference explains server-side Admin SDK operations that bypass Data Connect @auth checks. This is a valid administrative capability, but misuse can expose or modify data outside normal user authorization paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file states that Admin SDK operations run with elevated privileges and bypass @auth. This is expected Firebase behavior, so the risk is misuse rather than malicious skill intent.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, hardcoded IP, path traversal, and environment access findings are documentation examples or ordinary Firebase configuration. I found no executable Ruby code, no weak cryptographic implementation, and no malicious network or filesystem behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced Markdown examples for GraphQL, YAML, Firebase CLI, SDK output paths, UUID defaults, and emulator hosts. They do not execute when the skill is loaded.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production Deployment Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents a Firebase deploy command. This is useful for the workflow, but agents and users should confirm the active Firebase project before deployment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The deployment command is directly present in documentation. It is a legitimate command with real production side effects.

dangerous_patterns.title: Relative SDK Output Directories
dangerous_patterns.description: Several connector.yaml examples generate SDKs into relative parent directories such as web, Android, iOS, and Flutter app folders. This is normal project layout guidance, but it writes generated files outside the connector directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The relative paths are visible configuration examples. They are not traversal attacks, but generated SDK output can overwrite local files if paths are misconfigured.

content.value_statement: Designing Data Connect schemas and SDK setup can be slow and error-prone. This skill gives focused Firebase guidance for GraphQL models, operations, authorization, deployment, and client integration.
actual_capabilities: Drafts Data Connect schema.gql models with tables, relationships, indexes, defaults, and PostgreSQL-backed scalar types.
Creates GraphQL queries and mutations using generated Data Connect fields, filters, pagination, upserts, and transactions.
Adds authorization patterns with @auth, auth.uid filters, @check validation, and @redact guidance.
Explains dataconnect.yaml and connector.yaml setup, including service configuration and SDK output paths.
Guides SDK generation and client usage for Web, Android, iOS, Flutter, and Node Admin SDK workflows.
Provides examples for CRUD apps, user-owned resources, many-to-many relationships, search, and vector features.
limitations: It does not create or manage Firebase projects without user-approved commands and credentials.
It depends on Firebase CLI, Data Connect, Cloud SQL, and platform SDK behavior outside the skill files.
Generated examples still need review for each application data model and authorization policy.
Production deployment commands can affect real Firebase resources if the active project is wrong.

use_cases.title: Plan a Data Connect Backend
use_cases.description: Design PostgreSQL-backed GraphQL schemas, relationships, indexes, and defaults for a Firebase application.

use_cases.title: Secure App Data Access
use_cases.description: Add @auth rules, owner filters, role checks, validation, and redaction patterns to Data Connect operations.

use_cases.title: Integrate Generated SDKs
use_cases.description: Configure connector outputs and call generated operations from Web, Android, iOS, Flutter, or Node services.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Firebase Data Connect schema for a simple task app with users, tasks, due dates, status, and owner-based access.

prompt_templates.title: Add Queries and Mutations
prompt_templates.prompt: Write Data Connect queries and mutations for this schema. Include list, detail, create, update, delete, and upsert operations where useful.

prompt_templates.title: Review Authorization
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Data Connect operations for authorization gaps. Add @auth, auth.uid filters, @check rules, and redaction where needed.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Production Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan the dataconnect.yaml, connector.yaml, SDK generation outputs, emulator workflow, and deployment checklist for a multi-platform Firebase app.

output_examples.input: Design a movie review backend with public movie browsing and user-owned reviews.
output_examples.output: A schema plan with movies, actors, users, reviews, relationship tables, public read queries, authenticated review mutations, and owner filters.

output_examples.input: Make sure users can only edit their own notes.
output_examples.output: An authorization review that recommends USER-level access, auth.uid ownership filters, protected update and delete operations, and validation checks.

output_examples.input: Set up SDK generation for web, Android, iOS, and Flutter clients.
output_examples.output: A connector configuration plan with platform-specific output directories, package names, generation steps, and client initialization notes.

best_practices: Confirm the active Firebase project before running deploy, SDK generation, or emulator commands.
Use owner filters or role expressions in addition to broad USER authorization for sensitive records.
Keep generated SDK output paths explicit and review file changes before committing generated code.
anti_patterns: Do not use PUBLIC access for user-specific or sensitive data operations.
Do not rely on Admin SDK operations for client-facing workflows that need user authorization checks.
Do not deploy generated schemas or operations before reviewing indexes, constraints, and migration impact.

faq.question: What is Firebase Data Connect?
faq.answer: It is a Firebase service for PostgreSQL-backed apps with GraphQL schemas, generated operations, and type-safe client SDKs.

faq.question: Does this skill run Firebase commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides command guidance. Users and agents should confirm project context before running commands.

faq.question: Which client platforms are covered?
faq.answer: The references cover Web, Android, iOS, Flutter, and Node Admin SDK integration patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with authorization?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains @auth levels, CEL expressions, auth.uid filters, @check validation, and @redact usage.

faq.question: Does it replace Firebase documentation?
faq.answer: No. It is a focused workflow guide and example set. Teams should still verify current Firebase product behavior.

faq.question: What should I review before production deployment?
faq.answer: Review the active project, schema changes, generated SDK diffs, authorization rules, indexes, and Cloud SQL settings.
